name: Spanish B2 Grammar
description: Master CEFR B2 Spanish grammar — imperfect subjunctive (-ra and -se forms), compound subjunctive (haya hablado, hubiera hecho), si-clauses types 2 and 3, future and conditional perfect, passive voice (ser/se/estar), reported speech with tense backshift, advanced connectors, and the full por/para system. Fill in the blank to prove you know which form fits. Scoped from the Instituto Cervantes PCIC B2 curriculum.

facts.quizQuestion:
El médico le recomendó que {___} más.
(The doctor recommended that he rest more.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: The doctor recommended that he rest more.
facts.explanation: descansara — imperfect subjunctive of descansar (él/ella). Medical advice often uses recomendar/aconsejar que + imperfect subjunctive. Pattern: descansara, descansaras, descansara...

facts.quizQuestion:
Era mejor que {___} menos café.
(It was better that we drink less coffee.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: It was better that we drink less coffee.
facts.explanation: bebiéramos — imperfect subjunctive of beber (nosotros). -er nosotros form has a written accent: bebiéramos. After era mejor que (impersonal past trigger), use imperfect subjunctive.

facts.quizQuestion:
Si {___} más cuidadoso, no habrías tenido el accidente.
(If you were more careful, you wouldn't have had the accident.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If you were more careful, you wouldn't have had the accident.
facts.explanation: fueras — imperfect subjunctive of ser (tú). Mixed conditional: si + imperfect subj (past counterfactual fueras = if you had been) + conditional perfect (no habrías tenido). Ser/ir tú form: fueras.

facts.quizQuestion:
Si {___} más tiempo, haríamos más.
(If we had more time, we would do more.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If we had more time, we would do more.
facts.explanation: tuviéramos — imperfect subjunctive of tener (nosotros). Note the written accent: tuviéramos. Type 2 si-clause: si tuviéramos (nosotros form) + conditional haríamos.

facts.quizQuestion:
Si {___} más ejercicio, te sentirías mejor.
(If you exercised more, you would feel better.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If you exercised more, you would feel better.
facts.explanation: hicieras — imperfect subjunctive of hacer (tú). Type 2 si-clause with hacer. Si hicieras más ejercicio = if you exercised more. This is a common type 2 pattern with lifestyle advice.

facts.quizQuestion:
Ojalá {___} quedarnos más tiempo aquí.
(I wish we could stay here longer.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I wish we could stay here longer.
facts.explanation: pudiéramos — imperfect subjunctive of poder (nosotros). Ojalá + imperfect subjunctive expresses an unlikely wish. Nosotros form: pudiéramos (note accent).

facts.quizQuestion:
Espero que {___} bien a vuestra destino.
(I hope you (pl.) have arrived safely at your destination.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I hope you (pl.) have arrived safely at your destination.
facts.explanation: hayáis llegado — present perfect subjunctive, vosotros form. Hayáis = vosotros form of haber in present subjunctive. Used with vosotros (Spain only). After espero que + completed action.

facts.quizQuestion:
Si {___} ayuda, no habría fracasado.
(If he had asked for help, he would not have failed.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If he had asked for help, he would not have failed.
facts.grammarPointLabel: pluperfect subjunctive: hubiera + pedido — si-clause type 3
facts.explanation: hubiera pedido — pluperfect subjunctive, yo/él form. Type 3 si-clause: si hubiera pedido (he didn't ask, counterfactual) + no habría fracasado (conditional perfect). Pedido = past participle of pedir.

facts.quizQuestion:
Complete the type 2 si-clause: '{___}'
Meaning: If he wanted to, he could do it.
facts.sentenceTranslation: If he wanted to, he could do it.
facts.explanation: Si quisiera, podría hacerlo. Quisiera = imperfect subjunctive of querer. Podría = conditional of poder. Type 2: hypothetical present/future condition.

facts.correctAnswer: Si hubieras esperado, habrías podido venir.
acceptableAlternatives: Si hubieses esperado, habrías podido venir.
facts.quizQuestion:
Complete the type 3 si-clause: '{___}'
Meaning: If you had waited, you would have been able to come.
facts.sentenceTranslation: If you had waited, you would have been able to come.
facts.grammarPointLabel: si-clause type 3: si hubieras esperado... habrías podido
facts.explanation: Si hubieras esperado, habrías podido venir. Type 3: si + pluperfect subj (hubieras esperado — you didn't wait) + conditional perfect (habrías podido — you would have been able to).
facts.targetLanguageWord: Si hubieras esperado, habrías podido venir.

facts.quizQuestion:
Para el viernes, {___} la respuesta.
(By Friday, you will have received the answer.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: By Friday, you will have received the answer.
facts.explanation: habrás recibido — future perfect, tú form. Para el viernes = by Friday (deadline trigger). Habrás = tú form of haber in future. Recibido = past participle of recibir.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} antes, pero no sabía tu número.
(I would have called earlier, but I didn't know your number.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I would have called earlier, but I didn't know your number.
facts.grammarPointLabel: conditional perfect: habría + llamado (yo) — standalone with pero
facts.explanation: habría llamado — conditional perfect. Used without an explicit si-clause when the reason/condition is explained with pero (but). This shows a past action that didn't happen due to circumstances.

facts.quizQuestion:
El ladrón {___} por la policía.
(The thief was arrested by the police.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: The thief was arrested by the police.
facts.grammarPointLabel: ser + past participle passive: fue detenido (masc.) + por-agent
facts.explanation: fue detenido — ser passive (preterite). El ladrón is masculine: detenido. Agent: la policía introduced by por. Detenido = past participle of detener.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} trabajadores cualificados para el proyecto.
(Qualified workers are needed for the project.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Qualified workers are needed for the project.
facts.explanation: Se necesitan — se-passive with plural subject. Trabajadores (pl.) → necesitan (pl.). This is how job listings and notices express impersonal need in Spanish.

facts.quizQuestion:
Los libros {___} en la estantería por tema.
(The books are arranged on the shelf by topic.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: The books are arranged on the shelf by topic.
facts.grammarPointLabel: estar + past participle: están ordenados (masc. pl.)
facts.explanation: están ordenados — estar + past participle (resultative state). Los libros is masculine plural: ordenados (agreeing in gender and number). This describes the current arrangement as a state.

facts.quizQuestion:
Me contó que {___} a alguien muy interesante.
(Direct speech: 'He conocido a alguien muy interesante.' He told me he had met someone very interesting.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: He told me he had met someone very interesting.
facts.grammarPointLabel: reported speech backshift: present perfect → pluperfect (he conocido → había conocido)
facts.explanation: había conocido — in reported speech, present perfect (he conocido) shifts to pluperfect (había conocido). This is the same backshift as from the preterite: both shift to pluperfect in reported speech.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} sea difícil, lo intentaré.
(Even if it's difficult, I'll try.)
[Hint: unknown outcome — subjunctive follows]
facts.sentenceTranslation: Even if it's difficult, I'll try.
facts.grammarPointLabel: concessive connector: aunque + subjunctive (even if — hypothetical)
facts.explanation: aunque — with subjunctive (sea) = 'even if it's difficult' (we don't know yet). With indicative: aunque es difícil = 'even though it is difficult' (we know it's a fact). This aunque + subj/indic distinction is a core B2 skill.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} lo sabes, cuéntamelo todo.
(Since you know it, tell me everything.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Since you know it, tell me everything.
facts.explanation: ya que — 'since / seeing that'. Followed by indicative (lo sabes = you know it = a fact). Ya que is used when the cause is considered obvious or already known to the listener.

facts.quizQuestion:
No asistió a la reunión, {___} perdió información clave.
(He didn't attend the meeting, consequently he missed key information.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: He didn't attend the meeting, consequently he missed key information.
facts.explanation: por consiguiente — 'consequently / therefore'. Formal consecutive connector, more formal than así que. Common in academic writing and formal speech. Synonym: en consecuencia.

facts.quizQuestion:
No salgas {___} yo llegue.
(Don't leave until I arrive.)
[Hint: future event — subjunctive follows]
facts.sentenceTranslation: Don't leave until I arrive.
facts.grammarPointLabel: temporal connector: hasta que + subjunctive (until — future event)
facts.explanation: hasta que — 'until'. With subjunctive when referring to a future event: hasta que yo llegue = until I arrive (I haven't arrived yet). With indicative for past facts: esperé hasta que llegó = I waited until he arrived.

facts.quizQuestion:
Saldré {___} me lo impidan.
(I'll leave unless they prevent me.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I'll leave unless they prevent me.
facts.grammarPointLabel: conditional connector: a menos que + subjunctive (unless)
facts.explanation: a menos que — 'unless'. Always followed by subjunctive: a menos que me lo impidan (unless they prevent me). This is a negative condition: the main action will happen UNLESS the subordinate condition is met.

facts.quizQuestion:
Llegará {___} las cinco de la tarde.
(He'll arrive around five in the afternoon.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: He'll arrive around five in the afternoon.
facts.explanation: por — expressing approximate time. Por las cinco = around five o'clock. Contrast with para: para las cinco (by five o'clock = deadline). Por has the sense of 'somewhere around / approximately'.

facts.quizQuestion:
Era necesario que {___} a tiempo.
(It was necessary that we arrive on time.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: It was necessary that we arrive on time.
facts.explanation: llegásemos — imperfect subjunctive -se form of llegar (nosotros). The nosotros form has a written accent: llegásemos. This is the literary -se alternative to llegáramos.

facts.quizQuestion:
Era mejor que {___} más claramente.
(It was better that we speak more clearly.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: It was better that we speak more clearly.
facts.explanation: hablásemos — imperfect subjunctive -se form of hablar (nosotros). Written accent on á: hablásemos. The -se paradigm nosotros form always has a written accent on the penultimate syllable.

facts.quizQuestion:
Era bueno que {___} más verduras.
(It was good that we eat more vegetables.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: It was good that we eat more vegetables.
facts.explanation: comiésemos — imperfect subjunctive -se form of comer (nosotros). Written accent on é: comiésemos. The -se nosotros form: comiésemos.

facts.quizQuestion:
Quería que {___} más sobre historia.
(She wanted you to learn more about history.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: She wanted you to learn more about history.
facts.explanation: aprendieses — imperfect subjunctive -se form of aprender (tú). After quería que (past volition), use imperfect subjunctive. The tú -se form: aprendieses.

facts.correctAnswer: Si hablaras con él, lo entenderías.
acceptableAlternatives: Si hablases con él, lo entenderías.
facts.quizQuestion:
Complete the type 2 si-clause: '{___}'
Meaning: If you talked to him, you would understand.
facts.sentenceTranslation: If you talked to him, you would understand.
facts.grammarPointLabel: si-clause type 2: si hablaras... + lo entenderías
facts.explanation: Si hablaras con él, lo entenderías. Type 2 hypothetical: si + imperfect subjunctive (hablaras) + conditional (entenderías). This describes a hypothetical present: if you were to talk to him (but you're not).
facts.targetLanguageWord: Si hablaras con él, lo entenderías.

facts.correctAnswer: Si hubiera tenido tiempo, lo habría hecho.
acceptableAlternatives: Si hubiese tenido tiempo, lo habría hecho.
facts.quizQuestion:
Complete the type 3 si-clause: '{___}'
Meaning: If I had had time, I would have done it.
facts.sentenceTranslation: If I had had time, I would have done it.
facts.grammarPointLabel: si-clause type 3: si hubiera tenido... lo habría hecho
facts.explanation: Si hubiera tenido tiempo, lo habría hecho. Type 3: si + pluperfect subj (hubiera tenido — I didn't have time) + conditional perfect (habría hecho — I would have done it). Tenido = past participle of tener.
facts.targetLanguageWord: Si hubiera tenido tiempo, lo habría hecho.

facts.correctAnswer: Si no hubiera nevado ayer, haría menos frío hoy.
facts.quizQuestion:
Complete the mixed conditional: '{___}'
Meaning: If it hadn't snowed yesterday, it would be less cold today.
facts.sentenceTranslation: If it hadn't snowed yesterday, it would be less cold today.
facts.grammarPointLabel: mixed conditional: si no hubiera nevado (past) + haría menos frío (present)
facts.explanation: Si no hubiera nevado ayer, haría menos frío hoy. Mixed conditional: si + pluperfect subjunctive (past weather event that DID happen) + conditional simple (present consequence: it's cold now because it snowed).
facts.targetLanguageWord: Si no hubiera nevado ayer, haría menos frío hoy.

facts.correctAnswer: Si no hubiera venido, no te habría conocido.
facts.quizQuestion:
Complete the mixed conditional: '{___}'
Meaning: If I hadn't come, I wouldn't have met you.
facts.sentenceTranslation: If I hadn't come, I wouldn't have met you.
facts.grammarPointLabel: mixed conditional: si no hubiera venido (past) + no te habría conocido (past result)
facts.explanation: Si no hubiera venido, no te habría conocido. This is a type 3 si-clause (both past), but demonstrating how past counterfactuals can affect the present narrative. Type 3 result in conditional perfect.
facts.targetLanguageWord: Si no hubiera venido, no te habría conocido.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} apartamento amueblado en el centro.
(Furnished apartment for rent in the center.)
[Singular subject = singular verb]
facts.sentenceTranslation: Furnished apartment for rent in the center.
facts.explanation: Se alquila — se-passive with singular subject. Un apartamento = singular → alquila (singular). This is the classic rental advertisement pattern in Spanish. Contrast: se alquilan pisos (plural).

facts.quizQuestion:
Los acuerdos de paz {___} ayer.
(The peace agreements were signed yesterday.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: The peace agreements were signed yesterday.
facts.explanation: se firmaron — se-passive in preterite. Los acuerdos (pl.) → firmaron (pl.). This is equivalent to 'fueron firmados' but more natural in everyday Spanish.

facts.quizQuestion:
Le rogó que {___} un poco más.
(Direct speech: '¡Quédate!' She begged him to stay a little longer.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: She begged him to stay a little longer.
facts.grammarPointLabel: reported speech: rogar + imperfect subjunctive (quédate → se quedara)
facts.explanation: se quedara — rogar que + imperfect subjunctive. The reflexive imperative 'quédate' becomes 'que se quedara' in reported speech. Note the pronoun changes: te → se (to match él).

facts.quizQuestion:
Lo explicó claramente, {___} todos lo comprendieron.
(She explained it clearly, so that everyone understood.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: She explained it clearly, so that everyone understood.
facts.grammarPointLabel: consecutive connector: de manera que (so that / with the result that)
facts.explanation: de manera que — 'so that / in such a way that'. With indicative (todos comprendieron = they understood = real result). Synonymous with de modo que + indicative.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} vivas en mi casa, cumple las normas.
(While you live in my house, follow the rules.)
[Hint: ongoing future condition — subjunctive required]
facts.sentenceTranslation: While you live in my house, follow the rules.
facts.grammarPointLabel: temporal connector: mientras + subjunctive (while — ongoing future/conditional)
facts.explanation: mientras — 'while / as long as'. With subjunctive when it expresses an ongoing future condition: mientras vivas (as long as you live = future/conditional). With indicative: mientras estudiaba (while he was studying = past fact).

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} seas mayor, lo entenderás.
(When you are older, you will understand it.)
[Hint: future event — subjunctive follows]
facts.sentenceTranslation: When you are older, you will understand it.
facts.grammarPointLabel: temporal connector: cuando + subjunctive (future event — classic example)
facts.explanation: cuando — 'when' + subjunctive for future events. Cuando seas mayor = when you are older (future). The indicative would refer to a habit or past: cuando eras mayor = when you were older.

facts.correctAnswer: Si hubiera aceptado ese trabajo, estaría en París ahora.
facts.quizQuestion:
Complete the mixed conditional: '{___}'
Meaning: If I had accepted that job, I would be in Paris now.
(past si-clause + present consequence)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If I had accepted that job, I would be in Paris now.
facts.grammarPointLabel: mixed conditional: si hubiera aceptado (past) + estaría (present consequence)
facts.explanation: Si hubiera aceptado ese trabajo, estaría en París ahora. Mixed conditional: si + pluperfect subj (past: I didn't accept it) + conditional simple (present: I'm not in Paris). The key marker is 'ahora' (now) signaling a present state.
facts.targetLanguageWord: Si hubiera aceptado ese trabajo, estaría en París ahora.

facts.correctAnswer: Si hubiera nacido en España, hablaría español perfectamente.
facts.quizQuestion:
Complete the mixed conditional: '{___}'
Meaning: If I had been born in Spain, I would speak Spanish perfectly.
(past birth + present ability)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If I had been born in Spain, I would speak Spanish perfectly.
facts.grammarPointLabel: mixed conditional: si hubiera nacido (past) + hablaría (present consequence)
facts.explanation: Si hubiera nacido en España, hablaría español perfectamente. Mixed conditional: si + pluperfect subj (past counterfactual: I wasn't born there) + conditional simple (present ability: I would speak it). A classic mixed conditional with birth/origin.
facts.targetLanguageWord: Si hubiera nacido en España, hablaría español perfectamente.

facts.quizQuestion:
Quería que yo {___} con el director.
(She wanted me to talk to the director.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: She wanted me to talk to the director.
facts.explanation: hablara — imperfect subjunctive of hablar (yo). After quería que (past volition trigger), use the imperfect subjunctive. -ar pattern: hablara, hablaras, hablara, habláramos, hablarais, hablaran.

facts.quizQuestion:
Si {___} más despacio, te entendería mejor.
(If you spoke more slowly, I would understand you better.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If you spoke more slowly, I would understand you better.
facts.explanation: hablaras — imperfect subjunctive of hablar (tú). Type 2 si-clause: si + imperfect subjunctive. The -tú form of the -ra paradigm ends in -aras for -ar verbs.

facts.quizQuestion:
Me pidió que {___} con su jefe.
(He asked me to talk to his boss.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: He asked me to talk to his boss.
facts.explanation: hablara — imperfect subjunctive of hablar (yo/él/ella). After pedirle que (past request), use imperfect subjunctive. Note: yo and él/ella share the same form in the -ra paradigm.

facts.quizQuestion:
Me pidió que {___} a las nueve.
(He asked me to arrive at nine.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: He asked me to arrive at nine.
facts.explanation: llegara — imperfect subjunctive of llegar (yo). After pedir que with a past tense main verb, the subordinate clause uses the imperfect subjunctive. Spelling change: llegar → llegara (g→gu not needed in imperfect subj).

facts.quizQuestion:
Esperaba que {___} a tiempo.
(She hoped you would arrive on time.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: She hoped you would arrive on time.
facts.explanation: llegaras — imperfect subjunctive of llegar (tú). After esperaba que (past emotion/hope), use imperfect subjunctive. Tú form: llegaras.

facts.quizQuestion:
Era necesario que {___} más.
(It was necessary for them to work more.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: It was necessary for them to work more.
facts.explanation: trabajaran — imperfect subjunctive of trabajar (ellos/ellas). After era necesario que (impersonal + past), use imperfect subjunctive. Ellos/ellas form: -aran for -ar verbs.

facts.quizQuestion:
Me alegró que {___} ayer.
(I was glad you called yesterday.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I was glad you called yesterday.
facts.explanation: llamara — imperfect subjunctive of llamar. After me alegró que (past emotion), use imperfect subjunctive. The yo/él form is llamara.

facts.quizQuestion:
Quería que me {___}.
(I wanted you to help me.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I wanted you to help me.
facts.explanation: ayudaras — imperfect subjunctive of ayudar (tú). After quería que (past desire), use imperfect subjunctive. The tú form is ayudaras.

facts.quizQuestion:
Era necesario que {___} más.
(It was necessary for you to rest more.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: It was necessary for you to rest more.
facts.explanation: descansaras — imperfect subjunctive of descansar (tú). After era necesario que (impersonal + past), use imperfect subjunctive. Pattern: descansar → descansara, descansaras, descansara...

facts.quizQuestion:
Era importante que {___} para el examen.
(It was important that they study for the exam.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: It was important that they study for the exam.
facts.explanation: estudiaran — imperfect subjunctive of estudiar (ellos/ellas). Impersonal era importante que triggers imperfect subjunctive in a past-framed context. Ellos form: estudiaran.

facts.quizQuestion:
Era importante que {___} más claramente.
(It was important that we speak more clearly.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: It was important that we speak more clearly.
facts.explanation: habláramos — imperfect subjunctive of hablar (nosotros). The nosotros form has a written accent: habláramos. This distinguishes it from the conditional hablaríamos.

facts.quizQuestion:
Esperaba que {___} más duro.
(She hoped you would work harder.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: She hoped you would work harder.
facts.explanation: trabajaras — imperfect subjunctive of trabajar (tú). After esperaba que (past emotion), use the imperfect subjunctive. Tú form: trabajaras.

facts.quizQuestion:
Necesitaba que yo {___} algo.
(She needed me to eat something.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: She needed me to eat something.
facts.explanation: comiera — imperfect subjunctive of comer (yo). After necesitaba que (past necessity), use imperfect subjunctive. -er pattern: comiera, comieras, comiera, comiéramos, comierais, comieran.

facts.quizQuestion:
Si {___} menos, estaría más sano.
(If he ate less, he would be healthier.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If he ate less, he would be healthier.
facts.explanation: comiera — imperfect subjunctive of comer (él/ella). Type 2 si-clause uses si + imperfect subjunctive. The él/ella form of comer is comiera.

facts.quizQuestion:
Era importante que {___} cerca.
(It was important that we live nearby.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: It was important that we live nearby.
facts.explanation: viviéramos — imperfect subjunctive of vivir (nosotros). The nosotros form has a written accent: viviéramos. Note the double -i- pattern: viv-iera-mos.

facts.quizQuestion:
Si {___} más, encontrarías trabajo.
(If you learned more, you would find work.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If you learned more, you would find work.
facts.explanation: aprendieras — imperfect subjunctive of aprender (tú). Type 2 si-clause: si + imperfect subjunctive + conditional. The tú form of aprender is aprendieras.

facts.quizQuestion:
Me pidió que {___} una carta.
(She asked me to write a letter.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: She asked me to write a letter.
facts.explanation: escribiera — imperfect subjunctive of escribir (yo). After me pidió que (past request), use the imperfect subjunctive. Pattern for -ir: escribiera, escribieras, escribiera...

facts.quizQuestion:
Esperaba que {___} el libro antes de la clase.
(He hoped you would read the book before class.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: He hoped you would read the book before class.
facts.explanation: leyeras — imperfect subjunctive of leer (tú). Leer has a y-insertion: ley-era (like its preterite leyó). Pattern: leyera, leyeras, leyera, leyéramos, leyerais, leyeran.

facts.quizQuestion:
Necesitaba que {___} pronto del trabajo.
(She needed you to leave work soon.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: She needed you to leave work soon.
facts.explanation: salieras — imperfect subjunctive of salir (tú). Salir is regular in the imperfect subjunctive (uses the preterite stem: sali-). Pattern: saliera, salieras, saliera, saliéramos, salierais, salieran.

facts.quizQuestion:
Si {___} más rico, viajaría por el mundo.
(If I were richer, I would travel the world.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If I were richer, I would travel the world.
facts.explanation: fuera — imperfect subjunctive of ser (yo). Note: ser and ir share the same imperfect subjunctive forms (fuera/fuese). Context distinguishes meaning. Type 2 si-clause: si + imperfect subj.

facts.quizQuestion:
Ojalá {___} verdad.
(If only it were true.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If only it were true.
facts.explanation: fuera — imperfect subjunctive of ser (él/ella). Ojalá + imperfect subjunctive expresses an unlikely wish. Ser and ir share imperfect subjunctive forms: fuera, fueras, fuera, fuéramos, fuerais, fueran.

facts.quizQuestion:
Quería que {___} al médico.
(She wanted you to go to the doctor.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: She wanted you to go to the doctor.
facts.explanation: fueras — imperfect subjunctive of ir (tú). Ir uses the same forms as ser in the imperfect subjunctive. The tú form is fueras. After quería que (past volition), use imperfect subjunctive.

facts.quizQuestion:
Si {___} dinero, compraría un coche.
(If I had money, I would buy a car.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If I had money, I would buy a car.
facts.explanation: tuviera — imperfect subjunctive of tener (yo). Derived from the preterite 3pl: tuvieron → tuv- + -iera. Classic type 2 si-clause: si tuviera... + conditional. This is one of the most important B2 patterns.

facts.quizQuestion:
Necesitaba que {___} más paciencia.
(She needed you to have more patience.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: She needed you to have more patience.
facts.explanation: tuvieras — imperfect subjunctive of tener (tú). The stem tuv- comes from the preterite (tuvieron). Tú form: tuvieras.

facts.quizQuestion:
Quería que {___} los deberes.
(She wanted you to do the homework.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: She wanted you to do the homework.
facts.explanation: hicieras — imperfect subjunctive of hacer (tú). Stem from preterite: hicieron → hic- + -ieras. Note the spelling change c→c (stays) before -i-. Pattern: hiciera, hicieras, hiciera...

facts.quizQuestion:
Si {___} buen tiempo, saldríamos.
(If the weather were good, we would go out.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If the weather were good, we would go out.
facts.explanation: hiciera — imperfect subjunctive of hacer (él/ella). Type 2 si-clause with hacer as an impersonal weather verb. The stem hic- comes from the preterite hizo/hicieron.

facts.quizQuestion:
Si {___} aquí, te ayudaría.
(If he were here, he would help you.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If he were here, he would help you.
facts.explanation: estuviera — imperfect subjunctive of estar (él/ella). Stem from preterite: estuvieron → estuv- + -iera. Type 2 si-clause. Pattern: estuviera, estuvieras, estuviera, estuviéramos...

facts.quizQuestion:
Me pidió que {___} la verdad.
(He asked me to tell the truth.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: He asked me to tell the truth.
facts.explanation: dijera — imperfect subjunctive of decir (yo). Stem from preterite: dijeron → dij- + -era. After me pidió que (past request), use imperfect subjunctive. Pattern: dijera, dijeras, dijera...

facts.quizQuestion:
Ojalá {___} venir contigo.
(I wish I could come with you.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I wish I could come with you.
facts.explanation: pudiera — imperfect subjunctive of poder (yo). Stem from preterite: pudieron → pud- + -iera. Ojalá + imperfect subjunctive expresses an unlikely/impossible wish. Pattern: pudiera, pudieras, pudiera...

facts.quizQuestion:
Si {___} la respuesta, te la diría.
(If I knew the answer, I would tell you.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If I knew the answer, I would tell you.
facts.explanation: supiera — imperfect subjunctive of saber (yo). Stem from preterite: supieron → sup- + -iera. Classic si-clause type 2. This is a key B2 structure showing hypothetical knowledge.

facts.quizQuestion:
Quería que {___} a la fiesta.
(I wanted you to come to the party.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I wanted you to come to the party.
facts.explanation: vinieras — imperfect subjunctive of venir (tú). Stem from preterite: vinieron → vin- + -ieras. After quería que (past volition), use imperfect subjunctive. Pattern: viniera, vinieras, viniera...

facts.quizQuestion:
Ojalá {___} ayudar más.
(I wish he wanted to help more.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I wish he wanted to help more.
facts.explanation: quisiera — imperfect subjunctive of querer (él/ella). Stem from preterite: quisieron → quis- + -iera. Ojalá + imperfect subjunctive for an unlikely wish. Note: quisiera also means 'I would like' (polite conditional).

facts.quizQuestion:
Si {___} más despacio, lo entendería.
(If he spoke more slowly, I would understand.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If he spoke more slowly, I would understand.
facts.explanation: hablase — imperfect subjunctive -se form of hablar (él/ella). The -se form is the literary alternative to the -ra form (hablara). Both are equally correct. Pattern: hablase, hablases, hablase, hablásemos, hablaseis, hablasen.

facts.quizQuestion:
Esperaba que {___} a tiempo.
(He hoped they would arrive on time.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: He hoped they would arrive on time.
facts.explanation: llegasen — imperfect subjunctive -se form of llegar (ellos/ellas). Ellos/ellas form of the -se paradigm: -asen. Both llegasen and llegaran are correct.

facts.quizQuestion:
Era necesario que {___} antes de venir.
(It was necessary for you (pl.) to call before coming.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: It was necessary for you (pl.) to call before coming.
facts.explanation: llamaseis — imperfect subjunctive -se form of llamar (vosotros). The vosotros form ends in -aseis. This is the literary -se alternative to the -ra form llamarais.

facts.quizQuestion:
Era necesario que {___} más en equipo.
(It was necessary that we work more as a team.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: It was necessary that we work more as a team.
facts.explanation: trabajásemos — imperfect subjunctive -se form of trabajar (nosotros). Note the written accent on the á. The nosotros -se form always has a written accent: trabajásemos.

facts.quizQuestion:
El médico le dijo que {___} menos para estar más sano.
(The doctor told him to eat less to be healthier.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: The doctor told him to eat less to be healthier.
facts.explanation: comiese — imperfect subjunctive -se form of comer (él/ella). After le dijo que (reported speech / past indirect request), use imperfect subjunctive. -se paradigm: comiese, comieses, comiese, comiésemos, comieseis, comiesen.

facts.quizQuestion:
Quería que {___} más cerca.
(She wanted him to live closer.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: She wanted him to live closer.
facts.explanation: viviese — imperfect subjunctive -se form of vivir (él/ella). Pattern: viviese, vivieses, viviese, viviésemos, vivieseis, viviesen. Interchangeable with the -ra form viviera.

facts.quizQuestion:
Era importante que {___} pronto.
(It was important that they write soon.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: It was important that they write soon.
facts.explanation: escribiesen — imperfect subjunctive -se form of escribir (ellos/ellas). The ellos/ellas -se form ends in -iesen for -ir verbs: escribiesen.

facts.quizQuestion:
Si {___} posible, lo haría encantado.
(If it were possible, I would gladly do it.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If it were possible, I would gladly do it.
facts.explanation: fuese — imperfect subjunctive -se form of ser (él/ella). Ser and ir share the same imperfect subjunctive forms. -se paradigm: fuese, fueses, fuese, fuésemos, fueseis, fuesen. Interchangeable with fuera.

facts.quizQuestion:
Ojalá {___} más suerte.
(I wish I had more luck.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I wish I had more luck.
facts.explanation: tuviese — imperfect subjunctive -se form of tener (yo). Derived from preterite stem tuv-. -se paradigm: tuviese, tuvieses, tuviese, tuviésemos, tuvieseis, tuviesen.

facts.quizQuestion:
Si {___} venir, vendría encantado.
(If I could come, I would gladly come.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If I could come, I would gladly come.
facts.explanation: pudiese — imperfect subjunctive -se form of poder (yo). Derived from preterite stem pud-. -se form: pudiese. Interchangeable with pudiera.

facts.quizQuestion:
Quería que {___} la verdad.
(She wanted him to tell the truth.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: She wanted him to tell the truth.
facts.explanation: dijese — imperfect subjunctive -se form of decir (él/ella). Derived from preterite dij- stem. -se form: dijese. Pattern: dijese, dijeses, dijese, dijésemos, dijeseis, dijesen.

facts.quizQuestion:
Si {___} aquí, sería mejor.
(If he were here, it would be better.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If he were here, it would be better.
facts.explanation: estuviese — imperfect subjunctive -se form of estar (él/ella). Stem: estuv-. -se form: estuviese. Interchangeable with estuviera in all contexts.

facts.quizQuestion:
Espero que {___} bien.
(I hope you arrived safely.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I hope you arrived safely.
facts.explanation: hayas llegado — present perfect subjunctive (pretérito perfecto de subjuntivo). Formation: haya/hayas/haya/hayamos/hayáis/hayan + past participle. After espero que (present hope about a completed action), use present perfect subjunctive.

facts.quizQuestion:
No creo que {___} ya.
(I don't think he has finished yet.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I don't think he has finished yet.
facts.explanation: haya terminado — present perfect subjunctive. After no creo que (doubt trigger in present tense), use present perfect subjunctive for a completed action. The él/ella form of haber in present subjunctive is haya.

facts.quizQuestion:
Es posible que {___} ya.
(It's possible they have already left.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: It's possible they have already left.
facts.explanation: hayan salido — present perfect subjunctive. After es posible que (impersonal trigger), use present perfect subjunctive. Ellos/ellas form of haber in present subjunctive: hayan.

facts.quizQuestion:
Me alegra que {___}.
(I'm glad you have come.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I'm glad you have come.
facts.explanation: hayas venido — present perfect subjunctive. After me alegra que (present emotion about a completed action), use present perfect subjunctive. Venido is the irregular past participle of venir.

facts.quizQuestion:
Cuando {___}, llámame.
(When you have eaten, call me.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: When you have eaten, call me.
facts.grammarPointLabel: present perfect subjunctive: hayas + comido — cuando (temporal)
facts.explanation: hayas comido — present perfect subjunctive after cuando in a future temporal clause. When the action must be completed before something else happens, use present perfect subjunctive: cuando + hayas + past participle.

facts.quizQuestion:
Dudo que {___} eso en serio.
(I doubt he said that seriously.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I doubt he said that seriously.
facts.grammarPointLabel: present perfect subjunctive: haya + dicho (él/ella) — doubt
facts.explanation: haya dicho — present perfect subjunctive. After dudo que (doubt trigger), use present perfect subjunctive for a completed action. Dicho is the irregular past participle of decir.

facts.quizQuestion:
Es importante que {___} para entonces.
(It's important that we have finished by then.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: It's important that we have finished by then.
facts.explanation: hayamos terminado — present perfect subjunctive, nosotros form. After es importante que, use present perfect subjunctive to show that the action must be completed by a future point. Hayamos is the nosotros form of haber in present subjunctive.

facts.quizQuestion:
Espero que {___} a tiempo.
(I hope she arrived on time.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I hope she arrived on time.
facts.explanation: haya llegado — present perfect subjunctive, él/ella form. After espero que (present hope about a completed action), use present perfect subjunctive. The yo/él form of haber in present subjunctive is haya.

facts.quizQuestion:
No creo que {___} un error.
(I don't think we have made a mistake.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I don't think we have made a mistake.
facts.grammarPointLabel: present perfect subjunctive: hayamos + cometido (nosotros) — doubt
facts.explanation: hayamos cometido — present perfect subjunctive, nosotros form. After no creo que (doubt about a completed action), use present perfect subjunctive. Hayamos = nosotros form of haber in present subjunctive.

facts.quizQuestion:
Si {___} más, habría aprobado.
(If I had studied more, I would have passed.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If I had studied more, I would have passed.
facts.grammarPointLabel: pluperfect subjunctive: hubiera + estudiado — si-clause type 3
facts.explanation: hubiera estudiado — pluperfect subjunctive (pluscuamperfecto de subjuntivo). Formation: hubiera/hubiese + past participle. This is the if-clause of a type 3 (counterfactual past) conditional. Key B2 structure.

facts.quizQuestion:
Me alegró que {___} a la reunión.
(I was glad he had come to the meeting.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I was glad he had come to the meeting.
facts.grammarPointLabel: pluperfect subjunctive: hubiera + venido (él/ella) — past emotion
facts.explanation: hubiera venido — pluperfect subjunctive. After me alegró que (past emotion + completed action), use pluperfect subjunctive. The yo/él form of haber in imperfect subjunctive is hubiera.

facts.quizQuestion:
No creía que {___} ya.
(I didn't think they had already finished.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I didn't think they had already finished.
facts.grammarPointLabel: pluperfect subjunctive: hubieran + terminado (ellos/ellas) — past doubt
facts.explanation: hubieran terminado — pluperfect subjunctive. After no creía que (past doubt about a completed action), use pluperfect subjunctive. Hubieran is the ellos/ellas form.

facts.quizQuestion:
Si {___} antes, habrías visto la función.
(If you had arrived earlier, you would have seen the show.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If you had arrived earlier, you would have seen the show.
facts.grammarPointLabel: pluperfect subjunctive: hubieras + llegado (tú) — si-clause type 3
facts.explanation: hubieras llegado — pluperfect subjunctive, tú form. Si-clause type 3: si + pluperfect subj + conditional perfect. Hubieras is the tú form of hubiera (imperfect subjunctive of haber).

facts.quizQuestion:
Si lo {___}, no habría ido.
(If I had known, I would not have gone.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If I had known, I would not have gone.
facts.grammarPointLabel: pluperfect subjunctive: hubiera + sabido — si-clause type 3
facts.explanation: hubiera sabido — pluperfect subjunctive, yo form. Another type 3 si-clause: si + pluperfect subj (hubiera sabido) + conditional perfect (no habría ido). Sabido is the past participle of saber.

facts.quizQuestion:
Habla como si {___} en Madrid siempre.
(He speaks as if he had always lived in Madrid.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: He speaks as if he had always lived in Madrid.
facts.grammarPointLabel: pluperfect subjunctive: hubiera + vivido — como si (as if)
facts.explanation: hubiera vivido — pluperfect subjunctive after como si (as if). Como si always takes subjunctive: imperfect subjunctive for present comparisons, pluperfect subjunctive for past comparisons.

facts.quizQuestion:
Si {___} antes, habrían visto todo.
(If they had arrived earlier, they would have seen everything.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If they had arrived earlier, they would have seen everything.
facts.grammarPointLabel: pluperfect subjunctive: hubieran + llegado (ellos/ellas) — si-clause type 3
facts.explanation: hubieran llegado — pluperfect subjunctive, ellos/ellas form. Type 3 si-clause: si + pluperfect subj (hubieran llegado) + conditional perfect (habrían visto). Hubieran = ellos/ellas form.

facts.quizQuestion:
Complete the type 2 si-clause: '{___}'
Meaning: If I had more money, I would travel.
(si + imperfect subjunctive + conditional)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If I had more money, I would travel.
facts.explanation: Si tuviera más dinero, viajaría. Type 2 (hypothetical): si + imperfect subjunctive + conditional simple. Contrasts with type 1 (si tienes... irás) which uses present + future for likely conditions.

facts.correctAnswer: Si fuera más joven, correría más.
acceptableAlternatives: Si fuese más joven, correría más.
facts.quizQuestion:
Complete the type 2 si-clause: '{___}'
Meaning: If I were younger, I would run more.
(si + fuera/fuese + conditional)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If I were younger, I would run more.
facts.explanation: Si fuera más joven, correría más. Using fuera (imperfect subj of ser) in a type 2 si-clause. This is a hypothetical about present reality — impossible or unlikely from the speaker's perspective.
facts.targetLanguageWord: Si fuera más joven, correría más.

facts.correctAnswer: Si pudiera elegir, viviría en el campo.
acceptableAlternatives: Si pudiese elegir, viviría en el campo.
facts.quizQuestion:
Complete the type 2 si-clause: '{___}'
Meaning: If I could choose, I would live in the countryside.
facts.sentenceTranslation: If I could choose, I would live in the countryside.
facts.grammarPointLabel: si-clause type 2: si pudiera... — poder (hypothetical choice)
facts.explanation: Si pudiera elegir, viviría en el campo. Pudiera is the imperfect subjunctive of poder (yo/él). Type 2 si-clause showing an unlikely or hypothetical present condition.
facts.targetLanguageWord: Si pudiera elegir, viviría en el campo.

facts.correctAnswer: Si hiciera sol, iríamos a la playa.
acceptableAlternatives: Si hiciese sol, iríamos a la playa.
facts.quizQuestion:
Complete the type 2 si-clause: '{___}'
Meaning: If it were sunny, we would go to the beach.
facts.sentenceTranslation: If it were sunny, we would go to the beach.
facts.grammarPointLabel: si-clause type 2: si hiciera sol... — hacer (weather)
facts.explanation: Si hiciera sol, iríamos a la playa. Hiciera is the imperfect subjunctive of hacer (weather expression). The result clause iríamos is the conditional of ir (nosotros).
facts.targetLanguageWord: Si hiciera sol, iríamos a la playa.

facts.correctAnswer: Si hubiera estudiado más, habría aprobado.
acceptableAlternatives: Si hubiese estudiado más, habría aprobado.
facts.quizQuestion:
Complete the type 3 si-clause: '{___}'
Meaning: If I had studied more, I would have passed.
(si + pluperfect subj + conditional perfect)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If I had studied more, I would have passed.
facts.grammarPointLabel: si-clause type 3: si hubiera estudiado... habría aprobado
facts.explanation: Si hubiera estudiado más, habría aprobado. Type 3 (counterfactual past): si + pluperfect subjunctive + conditional perfect. This expresses a past condition that was NOT fulfilled.
facts.targetLanguageWord: Si hubiera estudiado más, habría aprobado.

facts.correctAnswer: Si hubiera llegado antes, habríamos podido hablar.
acceptableAlternatives: Si hubiese llegado antes, habríamos podido hablar.
facts.quizQuestion:
Complete the type 3 si-clause: '{___}'
Meaning: If he had arrived earlier, we could have talked.
facts.sentenceTranslation: If he had arrived earlier, we could have talked.
facts.grammarPointLabel: si-clause type 3: si hubiera llegado... habríamos podido
facts.explanation: Si hubiera llegado antes, habríamos podido hablar. Type 3 counterfactual: si + pluperfect subj + conditional perfect (habríamos podido = we would have been able to).
facts.targetLanguageWord: Si hubiera llegado antes, habríamos podido hablar.

facts.correctAnswer: Si lo hubiera pedido, te habría ayudado.
acceptableAlternatives: Si lo hubiese pedido, te habría ayudado.
facts.quizQuestion:
Complete the type 3 si-clause: '{___}'
Meaning: If you had asked for it, I would have helped you.
facts.sentenceTranslation: If you had asked for it, I would have helped you.
facts.grammarPointLabel: si-clause type 3: si lo hubiera pedido... habría ayudado
facts.explanation: Si lo hubiera pedido, te habría ayudado. Type 3: si + pluperfect subj + conditional perfect. Hubiera pedido = pluperfect subj of pedir. Habría ayudado = conditional perfect of ayudar.
facts.targetLanguageWord: Si lo hubiera pedido, te habría ayudado.

facts.correctAnswer: Si hubiera estudiado medicina, sería médico ahora.
facts.quizQuestion:
Complete the mixed conditional: '{___}'
Meaning: If I had studied medicine, I would be a doctor now.
(past si-clause + present consequence)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If I had studied medicine, I would be a doctor now.
facts.grammarPointLabel: mixed conditional: si hubiera + past + conditional present consequence
facts.explanation: Si hubiera estudiado medicina, sería médico ahora. Mixed conditional: si + pluperfect subjunctive (past counterfactual) + conditional simple (present consequence). The result is a present state that doesn't exist.
facts.targetLanguageWord: Si hubiera estudiado medicina, sería médico ahora.

facts.quizQuestion:
Para el lunes, {___} el proyecto.
(By Monday, I will have finished the project.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: By Monday, I will have finished the project.
facts.explanation: habré terminado — future perfect (futuro perfecto). Formation: habré/habrás/habrá/habremos/habréis/habrán + past participle. Used when an action will be completed before a specific future moment. Para el lunes is the deadline trigger.

facts.quizQuestion:
Cuando llegues, ya {___}.
(When you arrive, we will have already eaten.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: When you arrive, we will have already eaten.
facts.explanation: habremos comido — future perfect, nosotros form. When + future perfect: the eating will be completed before the arrival. Habremos = nosotros form of haber in future.

facts.quizQuestion:
Para las cinco, {___} de la oficina.
(By five o'clock, he will have left the office.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: By five o'clock, he will have left the office.
facts.explanation: habrá salido — future perfect, él/ella form. Salido is the past participle of salir. Para las cinco + future perfect indicates an action completed by a specified time in the future.

facts.quizQuestion:
Para el fin de año, {___} mucho.
(By the end of the year, I will have learned a lot.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: By the end of the year, I will have learned a lot.
facts.explanation: habré aprendido — future perfect, yo form. Para el fin de año + future perfect shows completion before a future deadline. Habré = yo form of haber in future tense.

facts.quizQuestion:
Con más ayuda, {___} antes.
(With more help, I would have finished sooner.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: With more help, I would have finished sooner.
facts.explanation: habría terminado — conditional perfect (condicional compuesto). Formation: habría/habrías/habría/habríamos/habríais/habrían + past participle. Used for hypothetical completed past actions.

facts.quizQuestion:
Si hubiera tenido tiempo, {___}.
(If I had had time, I would have come.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: If I had had time, I would have come.
facts.grammarPointLabel: conditional perfect: habría + venido (yo) — si-clause type 3 result
facts.explanation: habría venido — conditional perfect. The result clause of a type 3 si-clause uses the conditional perfect. Si hubiera tenido (plup. subj.) + habría venido (cond. perfect). Venido = past participle of venir.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___}, pero no tenía tu número.
(I would have called, but I didn't have your number.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I would have called, but I didn't have your number.
facts.explanation: habría llamado — conditional perfect used without an explicit si-clause. Pero introduces the reason. This shows a past action that didn't happen due to circumstances.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} de no ser por la lluvia.
(We would have gone if not for the rain.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: We would have gone if not for the rain.
facts.explanation: habríamos ido — conditional perfect, nosotros form. De no ser por + reason is an alternative conditional structure meaning 'if not for'. Habríamos = nosotros form of habría.

facts.quizQuestion:
Sin su apoyo, no {___} éxito.
(Without his support, I would not have had success.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Without his support, I would not have had success.
facts.grammarPointLabel: conditional perfect: habría + tenido (yo) — sin + conditional perfect
facts.explanation: habría tenido — conditional perfect. Sin (without) + conditional perfect describes what would have happened without a certain condition. Tenido = past participle of tener.

facts.quizQuestion:
El libro {___} por Cervantes.
(The book was written by Cervantes.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: The book was written by Cervantes.
facts.grammarPointLabel: ser + past participle passive: fue escrito
facts.explanation: fue escrito — ser passive in preterite (él/ella form). Structure: ser + past participle, agreeing with the subject (libro = masc. sing.). The agent introduced by por: por Cervantes.

facts.quizQuestion:
La carta {___} ayer.
(The letter was sent yesterday.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: The letter was sent yesterday.
facts.grammarPointLabel: ser + past participle passive: fue enviada (fem.)
facts.explanation: fue enviada — ser passive in preterite with feminine agreement. La carta is feminine, so the past participle agrees: enviada (not enviado). This agreement is essential in Spanish passive voice.

facts.quizQuestion:
La ciudad {___} en el siglo XII.
(The city was founded in the 12th century.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: The city was founded in the 12th century.
facts.grammarPointLabel: ser + past participle passive: fue fundada (fem.)
facts.explanation: fue fundada — ser passive (preterite) with feminine agreement. La ciudad is feminine, so: fue fundada. Historical facts often use ser passive to describe completed past actions.

facts.quizQuestion:
La ley {___} por el Parlamento.
(The law was approved by Parliament.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: The law was approved by Parliament.
facts.grammarPointLabel: ser + past participle passive: fue aprobada + por-agent
facts.explanation: fue aprobada — ser passive (preterite) + por-agent. La ley is feminine: aprobada. The agent (Parliament) is introduced by por. This is a complete passive construction with all components.

facts.quizQuestion:
Los documentos {___} por el director.
(The documents were signed by the director.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: The documents were signed by the director.
facts.grammarPointLabel: ser + past participle passive: fueron firmados (masc. pl.)
facts.explanation: fueron firmados — ser passive (preterite) with masculine plural agreement. Los documentos is masculine plural: firmados. Fueron = preterite of ser (ellos/ellas).

facts.quizQuestion:
La novela {___} a veinte idiomas.
(The novel was translated into twenty languages.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: The novel was translated into twenty languages.
facts.grammarPointLabel: ser + past participle passive: fue traducida (fem.)
facts.explanation: fue traducida — ser passive (preterite). La novela is feminine: traducida. Note: passive + a + number of languages is a common pattern in formal Spanish.

facts.quizQuestion:
El edificio {___} por arquitectos famosos.
(The building was built by famous architects.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: The building was built by famous architects.
facts.grammarPointLabel: ser + past participle passive: fue construido (masc.)
facts.explanation: fue construido — ser passive (preterite). El edificio is masculine: construido. The agent los arquitectos famosos is introduced by por.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} cinco idiomas en esa empresa.
(Five languages are spoken in that company.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Five languages are spoken in that company.
facts.grammarPointLabel: se-passive: se hablan (impersonal passive — plural subject)
facts.explanation: Se hablan — se-passive (pasiva refleja). When the agent is unknown or unimportant, use se + verb. The verb agrees with the subject (cinco idiomas = plural → hablan). This is more natural than the ser passive in everyday speech.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} pisos en este edificio.
(Apartments are sold in this building.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Apartments are sold in this building.
facts.explanation: Se venden — se-passive. Pisos is plural → verb is plural (venden). The se-passive is commonly used in notices, signs, and announcements. Compare: se vende un piso (singular).

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} tres puentes el año pasado.
(Three bridges were built last year.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Three bridges were built last year.
facts.explanation: Se construyeron — se-passive in preterite. Tres puentes = plural → construyeron (preterite plural). The se-passive can appear in any tense, agreeing with the noun subject.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} varios experimentos durante el estudio.
(Several experiments were carried out during the study.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Several experiments were carried out during the study.
facts.explanation: Se realizaron — se-passive in preterite. Varios experimentos = plural → realizaron. The se-passive is common in academic and formal writing to avoid naming the agent.

facts.quizQuestion:
La puerta {___} — no podemos entrar.
(The door is closed — we can't enter.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: The door is closed — we can't enter.
facts.grammarPointLabel: estar + past participle: está cerrada (resultative state)
facts.explanation: está cerrada — estar + past participle describes a resultative state (the current condition resulting from a past action). Contrast: la puerta fue cerrada (ser passive = the action of closing) vs. la puerta está cerrada (estar = the current state).

facts.quizQuestion:
El trabajo {___} — podemos irnos.
(The work is finished — we can leave.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: The work is finished — we can leave.
facts.grammarPointLabel: estar + past participle: está terminado (masc. — resultative)
facts.explanation: está terminado — estar + past participle. El trabajo is masculine: terminado. This describes the current state of completion, not the action of finishing (which would use ser).

facts.quizQuestion:
El contrato {___} — ya es oficial.
(The contract is signed — it's now official.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: The contract is signed — it's now official.
facts.grammarPointLabel: estar + past participle: está firmado (resultative state)
facts.explanation: está firmado — estar + past participle. Describes the present state after signing. El contrato is masculine: firmado. This is distinct from fue firmado (the action of signing happened).

facts.quizQuestion:
La mesa {___} para la cena.
(The table is set for dinner.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: The table is set for dinner.
facts.grammarPointLabel: estar + past participle: está puesta — irregular (poner)
facts.explanation: está puesta — estar + past participle of poner (irregular: puesto/puesta). La mesa is feminine: puesta. The table is currently in the state of being set, resulting from the action of setting it.

facts.quizQuestion:
Dijo que {___}.
(Direct speech: 'Estoy cansado.' He said he was tired.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: He said he was tired.
facts.explanation: estaba cansado — in reported speech with a past main verb (dijo), present tense shifts to imperfect. 'Estoy cansado' becomes 'que estaba cansado'. This is the most fundamental backshift rule.

facts.quizQuestion:
Me contó que {___}.
(Direct speech: 'Llegué tarde.' He told me he had arrived late.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: He told me he had arrived late.
facts.explanation: había llegado tarde — in reported speech, preterite shifts to pluperfect. 'Llegué tarde' → 'que había llegado tarde'. This shows the past-in-past relationship.

facts.quizQuestion:
Explicó que {___}.
(Direct speech: 'Vendré mañana.' He explained he would come the next day.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: He explained he would come the next day.
facts.explanation: vendría al día siguiente — in reported speech, future (vendré) shifts to conditional (vendría). Mañana becomes al día siguiente in formal backshift. Key B2 reported speech rule.

facts.quizQuestion:
Dijo que {___}.
(Direct speech: 'He terminado el trabajo.' She said she had finished the work.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: She said she had finished the work.
facts.grammarPointLabel: reported speech backshift: present perfect → pluperfect
facts.explanation: había terminado el trabajo — in reported speech, present perfect (ha terminado) shifts to pluperfect (había terminado). 'He terminado' → 'que había terminado'. Both preterite and present perfect shift to pluperfect.

facts.quizQuestion:
Nos contó que {___}.
(Direct speech: 'Vivo en Madrid.' He told us he lived in Madrid.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: He told us he lived in Madrid.
facts.grammarPointLabel: reported speech backshift: present → imperfect (vivo → vivía)
facts.explanation: vivía en Madrid — in reported speech, present (vivo) shifts to imperfect (vivía). 'Vivo en Madrid' → 'que vivía en Madrid'. The backshift shows the time distance from the reporting moment.

facts.quizQuestion:
Prometió que {___}.
(Direct speech: 'Estaré allí.' He promised he would be there.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: He promised he would be there.
facts.grammarPointLabel: reported speech backshift: future → conditional (estaré → estaría)
facts.explanation: estaría allí — in reported speech, future (estaré) shifts to conditional (estaría). 'Estaré allí' → 'que estaría allí'. Prometió que triggers the same backshift rules as other reporting verbs.

facts.quizQuestion:
Dijo que {___} esa tarde.
(Direct speech: 'No puedo venir.' She said she couldn't come that afternoon.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: She said she couldn't come that afternoon.
facts.grammarPointLabel: reported speech backshift: present → imperfect (no puedo → no podía)
facts.explanation: no podía venir — modal verb poder also backshifts in reported speech: no puedo → no podía. Esa tarde replaces esta tarde in backshifted time expressions.

facts.quizQuestion:
Le dijo que {___}.
(Direct speech: '¡Ven inmediatamente!' He told him to come immediately.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: He told him to come immediately.
facts.grammarPointLabel: reported speech: imperative → imperfect subjunctive (ven → viniera)
facts.explanation: viniera inmediatamente — in reported speech, imperatives become imperfect subjunctive. '¡Ven!' → 'que viniera'. The verb is decir que (not pedir que, but the pattern is the same). Venir: imperative ven → imperfect subj viniera.

facts.quizQuestion:
Le pidió que {___}.
(Direct speech: '¡Ayúdame!' She asked him to help her.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: She asked him to help her.
facts.grammarPointLabel: reported speech: pedir + imperfect subjunctive (ayuda → ayudara)
facts.explanation: le ayudara — pedir que + imperfect subjunctive. Pedir que is the reporting verb for requests. The imperative 'ayúdame' becomes 'que le ayudara' in reported speech (imperfect subjunctive + pronoun change).

facts.quizQuestion:
Le aconsejé que {___}.
(Direct speech: '¡Descansa!' I advised him to rest.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I advised him to rest.
facts.grammarPointLabel: reported speech: aconsejar + imperfect subjunctive (descansa → descansara)
facts.explanation: descansara — aconsejar que + imperfect subjunctive. Aconsejar que (to advise that) uses the same pattern as pedir que. The imperative 'descansa' becomes 'que descansara' in reported speech.

facts.quizQuestion:
Le ordenó que {___}.
(Direct speech: '¡Sal de la sala!' He ordered her to leave the room.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: He ordered her to leave the room.
facts.grammarPointLabel: reported speech: ordenar + imperfect subjunctive (sal → saliera)
facts.explanation: saliera de la sala — ordenar que + imperfect subjunctive. The imperative 'sal' (tú) of salir becomes 'que saliera' in reported speech. Salir → imperfect subj: saliera.

facts.quizQuestion:
Me pidió que {___}.
(Direct speech: '¡No digas nada!' He asked me not to say anything.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: He asked me not to say anything.
facts.grammarPointLabel: reported speech: pedir + negative imperfect subjunctive (no digas → no dijera)
facts.explanation: no dijera nada — negative reported imperative. '¡No digas nada!' (negative tú imperative) becomes 'que no dijera nada' in reported speech. Decir: imperfect subj dijera.

facts.quizQuestion:
Le pidió que {___}.
(Direct speech: '¡Habla más despacio!' She asked him to speak more slowly.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: She asked him to speak more slowly.
facts.grammarPointLabel: reported speech: pedir + imperfect subjunctive (habla → hablara)
facts.explanation: hablara más despacio — pedir que + imperfect subjunctive. The affirmative tú imperative 'habla' → reported speech 'que hablara'. Hablar: imperfect subj hablara.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} llueva, iremos al partido.
(Even if it rains, we'll go to the match.)
[Hint: unknown/hypothetical — use subjunctive form after blank]
facts.sentenceTranslation: Even if it rains, we'll go to the match.
facts.explanation: aunque — concessive connector meaning 'even if' (with subjunctive) or 'even though' (with indicative). Aunque + subjunctive when the situation is unknown or hypothetical. Aunque llueva = even if it rains (we don't know if it will).

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} estaba cansada, siguió trabajando.
(Even though she was tired, she kept working.)
[Hint: known fact — indicative follows]
facts.sentenceTranslation: Even though she was tired, she kept working.
facts.explanation: aunque — 'even though' with indicative when the situation is a known fact. Aunque estaba cansada (we know she was tired = indicative). Contrast: aunque esté cansada (even if she is — hypothetical = subjunctive).

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} estudies, no aprenderás en un día.
(No matter how much you study, you won't learn in a day.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: No matter how much you study, you won't learn in a day.
facts.grammarPointLabel: concessive connector: por mucho que + subjunctive
facts.explanation: Por mucho que — 'no matter how much' + subjunctive. Always followed by subjunctive. Contrast: por mucho que estudies (subj) = no matter how much you study. Equivalent: por más que estudies.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} estaba cansado, siguió trabajando.
(Despite being tired, he kept working.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Despite being tired, he kept working.
facts.grammarPointLabel: concessive connector: a pesar de que + indicative (known concession)
facts.explanation: A pesar de que — 'despite the fact that / despite + -ing'. Followed by indicative when the situation is factual. A pesar de (without que) is followed by a noun or infinitive: a pesar de la lluvia / a pesar de estar cansado.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} no tienes dinero, te presto yo.
(Since you don't have money, I'll lend you some.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Since you don't have money, I'll lend you some.
facts.explanation: Puesto que — formal causal connector meaning 'since / given that'. Always followed by indicative. More formal than ya que or porque. Common in written and formal speech.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} estás aquí, ayúdame con esto.
(Since you're here, help me with this.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Since you're here, help me with this.
facts.explanation: ya que — causal connector meaning 'since / seeing that'. Followed by indicative. Less formal than puesto que. Common in everyday speech when giving a reason.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} no hay más preguntas, concluimos la reunión.
(Given that there are no more questions, we'll conclude the meeting.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Given that there are no more questions, we'll conclude the meeting.
facts.explanation: Dado que — 'given that / since'. Formal causal connector, always with indicative. Used in formal/professional contexts. Similar to puesto que but slightly more formal.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} la lluvia, cancelamos el partido.
(Due to the rain, we cancelled the match.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Due to the rain, we cancelled the match.
facts.grammarPointLabel: causal connector: debido a (due to + noun)
facts.explanation: Debido a — 'due to / because of'. Followed by a noun (not a clause): debido a la lluvia. With a clause: debido a que + indicative. It's a formal causal expression.

facts.quizQuestion:
No estudié, {___} suspendí el examen.
(I didn't study, therefore I failed the exam.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I didn't study, therefore I failed the exam.
facts.explanation: por lo tanto — 'therefore / thus / consequently'. Consecutive connector showing that the second clause results from the first. Can be placed at the start of the second clause with a comma before it.

facts.quizQuestion:
Llegué tarde, {___} me perdí el principio.
(I arrived late, so I missed the beginning.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I arrived late, so I missed the beginning.
facts.explanation: así que — 'so / therefore'. Informal consecutive connector. More common in spoken Spanish than por lo tanto. The second clause shows the direct result or consequence of the first.

facts.quizQuestion:
Habló despacio, {___} todos le entendieron.
(He spoke slowly, so that everyone understood him.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: He spoke slowly, so that everyone understood him.
facts.grammarPointLabel: consecutive connector: de modo que (so that / with the result that)
facts.explanation: de modo que — 'so that / with the result that'. With indicative: shows the actual result achieved. With subjunctive: shows intended purpose (like para que). De modo que + indicative emphasizes the result.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} llegues, llámame.
(As soon as you arrive, call me.)
[Hint: future temporal — subjunctive follows]
facts.sentenceTranslation: As soon as you arrive, call me.
facts.explanation: en cuanto — 'as soon as'. Followed by subjunctive when referring to a future event. En cuanto llegues (subj) = as soon as you arrive. With past events: en cuanto llegó (indicative) = as soon as he arrived.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} hayas terminado, puedes irte.
(As soon as you have finished, you can go.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: As soon as you have finished, you can go.
facts.grammarPointLabel: temporal connector: tan pronto como + present perfect subjunctive
facts.explanation: Tan pronto como — 'as soon as'. Like en cuanto, followed by subjunctive for future events. Here hayas terminado = present perfect subjunctive (the finishing must happen before leaving). Interchangeable with en cuanto.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} hayas comido, descansa un poco.
(When you have eaten, rest a little.)
[Hint: future event — subjunctive required]
facts.sentenceTranslation: When you have eaten, rest a little.
facts.explanation: cuando — 'when'. With indicative for past/present facts: cuando llegó (when he arrived). With subjunctive for future events: cuando hayas comido (when you have eaten — future). This cuando + subj rule is a key B1/B2 point.

facts.quizQuestion:
Vendré {___} llueva.
(I'll come unless it rains.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I'll come unless it rains.
facts.grammarPointLabel: conditional connector: a menos que + subjunctive (unless)
facts.explanation: a menos que — 'unless'. Always followed by subjunctive. A menos que llueva = unless it rains. The subjunctive is mandatory after a menos que regardless of tense.

facts.quizQuestion:
Puedes ir {___} vuelvas pronto.
(You can go as long as you come back soon.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: You can go as long as you come back soon.
facts.grammarPointLabel: conditional connector: con tal de que + subjunctive (as long as)
facts.explanation: con tal de que — 'as long as / provided that'. Always followed by subjunctive. Con tal de que vuelvas = as long as you come back. The condition is required for the main action to be permitted.

facts.quizQuestion:
Lleva paraguas {___} llueva.
(Take an umbrella in case it rains.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: Take an umbrella in case it rains.
facts.grammarPointLabel: conditional connector: en caso de que + subjunctive (in case)
facts.explanation: en caso de que — 'in case'. Always followed by subjunctive. En caso de que llueva = in case it rains. Used for precautions against possible (but not expected) events.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} estudies, aprobarás.
(As long as you study, you'll pass.)
[Hint: conditional 'as long as' + subjunctive]
facts.sentenceTranslation: As long as you study, you'll pass.
facts.grammarPointLabel: conditional connector: siempre que + subjunctive (as long as)
facts.explanation: siempre que — 'as long as / whenever' + subjunctive. Siempre que estudies = as long as you study. When used conditionally (as long as), it takes subjunctive. When factual (every time/whenever), it takes indicative.

facts.quizQuestion:
Lo hizo {___} amor, no {___} dinero.
(He did it for love, not for money.)
[Both blanks: same preposition]
facts.sentenceTranslation: He did it for love, not for money.
facts.explanation: por — expressing cause or motive. Por amor = for (the cause of) love. Por dinero = for (the motive of) money. Key rule: por expresses the reason/cause behind an action (looking backward at the cause).

facts.quizQuestion:
El libro fue escrito {___} un autor famoso.
(The book was written by a famous author.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: The book was written by a famous author.
facts.explanation: por — the agent in a passive construction is introduced by por. El libro fue escrito por... = The book was written by... This is distinct from para which would indicate for (recipient/purpose).

facts.quizQuestion:
Lo cambié {___} otro modelo más nuevo.
(I exchanged it for a newer model.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I exchanged it for a newer model.
facts.explanation: por — expressing exchange or trade. Cambiar X por Y = to exchange X for Y. Lo cambié por otro = I exchanged it for another. Also: lo vendí por 50 euros = I sold it for 50 euros.

facts.quizQuestion:
Estudié {___} tres horas sin parar.
(I studied for three hours without stopping.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I studied for three hours without stopping.
facts.explanation: por — expressing duration (how long something lasted). Estudié por tres horas = I studied for three hours. Contrast with para which expresses a deadline: lo necesito para el lunes = I need it by Monday.

facts.quizQuestion:
Pasamos {___} el centro de la ciudad.
(We passed through the city center.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: We passed through the city center.
facts.explanation: por — expressing movement through or along a place. Pasar por = to pass through. Caminar por la playa = to walk along the beach. Contrast: para Madrid = headed to/toward Madrid (destination).

facts.quizQuestion:
Gano veinte euros {___} hora.
(I earn twenty euros per hour.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I earn twenty euros per hour.
facts.grammarPointLabel: por: rate/per (euros por hora, tres veces por semana)
facts.explanation: por — expressing rate or frequency. Veinte euros por hora = twenty euros per hour. Tres veces por semana = three times per week. This is the 'per' sense of por: a fixed quantity per unit.

facts.quizQuestion:
Firmé el documento {___} él, porque estaba enfermo.
(I signed the document on his behalf, because he was sick.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I signed the document on his behalf, because he was sick.
facts.grammarPointLabel: por: on behalf of / in place of
facts.explanation: por — expressing acting on behalf of or in place of someone. Firmé por él = I signed for/instead of him. Habló por todos = she spoke for everyone. This distinguishes from para (purpose/recipient): firmé para él = I signed it for him (as a gift).

facts.quizQuestion:
Te llamé tres veces {___} teléfono.
(I called you three times by phone.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I called you three times by phone.
facts.grammarPointLabel: por: communication medium (por teléfono, por correo)
facts.explanation: por — expressing the medium or means of communication. Por teléfono = by phone. Por correo = by mail. Por internet = via the internet. Por email = by email. This is a fixed expression pattern.

facts.quizQuestion:
Estudio {___} aprender, no {___} aprobar.
(I study to learn, not to pass.)
[Both blanks: same preposition]
facts.sentenceTranslation: I study to learn, not to pass.
facts.explanation: para — expressing purpose or goal. Para + infinitive = in order to. Estudio para aprender = I study in order to learn. This is the most basic para usage: the purpose of an action.

facts.quizQuestion:
Esta carta es {___} María.
(This letter is for María.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: This letter is for María.
facts.explanation: para — expressing the recipient (who something is intended for). Esta carta es para María = This letter is for María (she will receive it). Compare por: Esta carta fue escrita por María (por = agent who wrote it).

facts.quizQuestion:
Salgo {___} Madrid mañana por la mañana.
(I'm leaving for Madrid tomorrow morning.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I'm leaving for Madrid tomorrow morning.
facts.explanation: para — expressing destination when there is intentional movement toward a goal. Salgo para Madrid = I'm leaving for/toward Madrid. Compare por: pasé por Madrid = I passed through Madrid.

facts.quizQuestion:
Lo necesito {___} el lunes a primera hora.
(I need it by Monday first thing.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: I need it by Monday first thing.
facts.grammarPointLabel: para: deadline (para el lunes = by Monday)
facts.explanation: para — expressing a deadline (by/by the time of). Para el lunes = by Monday. This is a key para vs. por distinction: para lunes (deadline) vs. por lunes (approximate time: around Monday).

facts.quizQuestion:
{___}, es demasiado difícil este problema.
(For me / In my opinion, this problem is too difficult.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: For me, this problem is too difficult.
facts.explanation: Para mí — 'for me / in my opinion'. Para + pronoun expresses opinion or viewpoint. Para mí, para ti, para él... Compare with: a mí me parece (expressing how something seems to you).

facts.quizQuestion:
{___} principiante, habla muy bien el español.
(For a beginner, she speaks Spanish very well.)
[Hint: unexpected contrast — para + ser + noun]
facts.sentenceTranslation: For a beginner, she speaks Spanish very well.
facts.grammarPointLabel: para: unexpected contrast (para ser... = for a/an...)
facts.explanation: Para ser — expressing unexpected contrast. Para ser principiante = for a beginner (given that she is a beginner, her level is surprisingly good). This is a sophisticated para usage expressing contrast between expectation and reality.

facts.quizQuestion:
Trabaja {___} una empresa internacional en Barcelona.
(She works for an international company in Barcelona.)
facts.sentenceTranslation: She works for an international company in Barcelona.
facts.grammarPointLabel: para: employment (trabajar para = work for an employer)
facts.explanation: para — expressing employment relationship (who you work for). Trabaja para una empresa = she works for a company (she is employed by them). Compare por: trabajó por él = he worked in his place / on his behalf.

chainThemes.name: Imperfect Subjunctive
chainThemes.description: Imperfecto de subjuntivo — both -ra and -se variants, regular and irregular (fuera, tuviera, hiciera, dijera, pudiera)

chainThemes.name: Compound Subjunctive
chainThemes.description: Present perfect subjunctive (haya hablado) + pluperfect subjunctive (hubiera/hubiese hablado)

chainThemes.name: Si-Clauses: Full System
chainThemes.description: Type 2 hypothetical (si tuviera... haría) + Type 3 counterfactual (si hubiera tenido... habría hecho) + mixed conditionals

chainThemes.name: Compound Tenses
chainThemes.description: Future perfect (habré hecho) + conditional perfect (habría hecho) — compound tense formation and uses

chainThemes.name: Passive Voice
chainThemes.description: Ser + past participle (action passive) + se-passive (impersonal) + estar + past participle (resultative state)

chainThemes.name: Reported Speech
chainThemes.description: Estilo indirecto — tense backshift rules + reporting verbs + imperative becomes imperfect subjunctive

chainThemes.name: Advanced Connectors
chainThemes.description: Concessive (aunque + subj, por mucho que) + causal (puesto que, dado que) + consecutive (por lo tanto, así que) + temporal/conditional with subjunctive

chainThemes.name: Por vs. Para: Advanced
chainThemes.description: Full por/para system — cause, agent, exchange, duration, through, per vs. purpose, recipient, destination, deadline, opinion

answerTypePools.description: Imperfect subjunctive -ra forms: regular -ar verbs (hablara, llegaras, trabajaran...)
Imperfect subjunctive -ra forms: regular -er/-ir verbs (comiera, viviera, aprendiera...)
Imperfect subjunctive -ra forms: irregular verbs (fuera, tuviera, hiciera, dijera, pudiera, supiera, pusiera, viniera, quisiera, estuviera)
Imperfect subjunctive -se forms: regular -ar verbs (hablase, llegases, trabajasen...)
Imperfect subjunctive -se forms: regular -er/-ir verbs (comiese, viviese, aprendiese...)
Imperfect subjunctive -se forms: irregular verbs (fuese, tuviese, hiciese, dijese, pudiese, supiese, pusiese, viniese, quisiese, estuviese)
Present perfect subjunctive: haya/hayas/haya/hayamos/hayáis/hayan forms in context
Pluperfect subjunctive: hubiera/hubiese + past participle constructions
answerTypePools.homogeneityExemptNote: Mixed compound constructions: hubiera + various past participles; inherent length variation

answerTypePools.description: Si-clause type 2 hypothetical: si + imperfect subjunctive + conditional (full sentences)
syntheticDistractors: si tienes dinero, compras un coche
si tendrías dinero, comprarías un coche
si tengas dinero, comprarías un coche
si has tenido dinero, comprarás un coche
si tenías dinero, compraste un coche
si tendrás dinero, comprarás un coche
si tendría más tiempo, estudiaría más
si supiste la respuesta, la dijiste
answerTypePools.homogeneityExemptNote: Full conditional sentences with inherent length variation between subjects and verbs

answerTypePools.description: Si-clause type 3 counterfactual past: si + pluperfect subjunctive + conditional perfect
syntheticDistractors: si había estudiado más, habría aprobado
si habría llegado antes, habría visto
si hubiera llegado antes, había visto
si hubiese pedido, me habrías ayudado
answerTypePools.homogeneityExemptNote: Full counterfactual sentences with inherent length variation

answerTypePools.description: Mixed conditionals: past si-clause with present consequence
syntheticDistractors: si hubiera estudiado medicina, habría sido médico
si estudiaría medicina, sería médico ahora
si hubiera comido menos, estaría sano
si habría nevado ayer, hará menos frío hoy
si hubiera nevado ayer, haría menos frío ahora
si no habría venido, no habría conocido
si no hubieras ido, serías más feliz ahora
si hubiera dicho la verdad, todo estaría mejor
si hubiese trabajado más, estaría en una mejor posición
si no hubiera llovido, no estaríamos mojados
answerTypePools.homogeneityExemptNote: Mixed conditional sentences have inherent structural length variation

answerTypePools.description: Future perfect: habré/habrás/habrá/habremos/habréis/habrán forms in context
Conditional perfect: habría/habrías/habría/habríamos/habríais/habrían forms in context
Past participle forms in ser-passive (written, sent, built, signed, founded...)
Se-passive constructions showing impersonal passive in various tenses
answerTypePools.homogeneityExemptNote: Se-passive constructions vary in length due to different subject nouns and tense forms

answerTypePools.description: Past participle forms with estar showing resultative state (cerrada, terminado, firmado...)
Reported speech: backshifted verb forms in indirect speech context
answerTypePools.homogeneityExemptNote: Indirect speech constructions vary in length due to different verbs and context

answerTypePools.description: Reported imperatives as imperfect subjunctive constructions
syntheticDistractors: me pidió que no diga nada
answerTypePools.homogeneityExemptNote: These constructions vary in length due to different reporting verbs and content

answerTypePools.description: Concessive connectors: aunque, a pesar de que, por mucho que, por más que, aun cuando
Causal connectors: puesto que, ya que, dado que, debido a (que), visto que
Consecutive connectors: por lo tanto, así que, de modo que, de manera que, por consiguiente, en consecuencia
Temporal connectors with subjunctive for future events: en cuanto, tan pronto como, cuando, hasta que, mientras, después de que
Conditional connectors with subjunctive: a menos que, con tal de que, siempre que, en caso de que, a condición de que
Por in advanced roles: cause, passive agent, exchange, duration, through/along, per/rate, on behalf of, medium
Para in advanced roles: purpose, recipient, destination, deadline, opinion, unexpected contrast, employment
